title: not the old microsoft
pros: Microsoftâs performance review process gives a clear, structured way to talk about impact. It typically encourages you to tie your work to measurable outcomes (delivery, quality, customer value, collaboration), which makes accomplishments easier to explain and helps managers compare contributions consistently across a team. When done well, it also creates a regular rhythm for feedback and growthâso youâre not surprised at year-endâand can help you align on priorities, scope, and career development steps.
cons: The downside is that the experience can feel inconsistent depending on the org, manager, and calibration dynamics. Even with guidelines, expectations may be interpreted differently across teams, and outcomes can sometimes feel influenced by visibility, narrative-writing skill, or shifting priorities rather than purely day-to-day contribution. The process can also add significant overhead (self-assessments, evidence gathering, review writing), and if feedback isnât frequent during the year, the review may feel more like a paperwork exercise than a genuinely helpful development tool.

title: Xbox: Layoff Culture, Chaotic Leadership
pros: Your coworkers at low levels will be great! Most of the people are awesome, competent, and dependable. Products are fun to work with.
cons: Xbox has fully transitioned to a layoff culture where no one knows how long they will be employed for or at what point further layoffs will be announced. Many liken in it to a party with friends on the Titanic. Leadership expectations are unrealistic and fully uncommunicated. Many teams and orgs have competing goals. Leadership is stingy with advancement and promotions, often promoting the least deserving (often via nepotism). Over 50% of Xbox's workforce is vendor and contract work, so the majority of workers are underpaid and over tasked. "Do more with less" is standard operational mentality.

title: A Good Place to Start and Grow
body: A Good Place to Start and Grow
pros: The company provides a good work-life balance with excellent benefits. The engineering culture encourages learning and offers access to cutting-edge technology. My team is supportive and collaboration is generally strong. There are many internal resources and training programs available for career development. The company brand is strong and looks great on a resume.
cons: Work can sometimes feel siloed within large product groups. The promotion process for engineers can be opaque and slow. Bureaucracy can hinder decision-making and slow down projects. Compensation, while good, is often matched or exceeded by competing tech companies. As a new hire, it can be challenging to understand the scope and impact of your projects initially.

cons: Very corporate, cutthroat, especially lately. They kicked off the latest round of layoffs with zero severance âperformance basedâ layoffs. Corporatist crap. Really just a way to dodge paying severance, and even cut of health benefits mid month. This one left a lot of families high and try to save a buck. Thereâs way too many middle managers jockeying for promotions and patting themselves on the back instead of actually building things people are going to use. Itâs also very much not one Microsoft (despite what Satya says on camera). You can access far more or less resources and opportunities depending on where you are in the company. Give me the makeup of a team (how many âseniorâ or âprincipleâ positions theyâre allocated) and you can tell where in the company they are.

body: Strong compensation, challenging work, but high expectations
pros: Competitive total compensation, strong stock grants, good benefits, and opportunities to work on large-scale, impactful systems. Smart coworkers, solid engineering culture, and exposure to complex real-world problems. Career growth is possible if you are proactive and visible.
cons: High expectations and workload can lead to burnout. Ambiguity is common, priorities can shift quickly, and success often depends on navigating org structure and visibility. Work-life balance varies significantly by team and manager.

title: Specialist
cons: â Cons of Working at Microsoft 1. Slower Pace Compared to Startups Decision-making can be slow due to scale. Many layers of approval. 2. Bureaucracy & Processes Heavy documentation, compliance, and internal tooling. Can feel restrictive for highly entrepreneurial people. 3. Impact Can Feel Indirect At a very large company, individual contributions may feel diluted. Some teams work on incremental improvements rather than breakthroughs. 4. Team Experience Varies A Lot Culture and workload depend heavily on: Manager Org (Azure vs Gaming vs Research) Same company, very different experiences. 5. High Bar for Entry Interviews are difficult (system design, coding, behavioral). Strong competition from top global talent. 6. Stock Growth Can Be Cyclical RSU value depends on market conditions. Less explosive upside than early-stage startups. 7. Less Exposure to Business/Client Strategy Compared to consulting roles, less client-facing and strategic work.

title: Great
cons: Salary is a little lower often than other big tech companies, as well as bonuses and shares/stock. It's still good, but often other big tech can be higher (Google, Meta, maybe Amazon or Oracle, depending on the role, etc.). Similarly, promotions are sometimes a little harder to get, and you can't get a promo by transfer to another team (very rare). the review process is a little too time consuming (comparatively, but it's close). Although the Hackathons are nice, it's not really built into the culture much year round (so it might take some effort to do anything effective through that, in finding the right team, etc.). They don't have free food (which is a minimal factor; some big tech do), but their free drinks are nice, and there are plenty of catered events and meetings.

title: CSAM Requirements and Expectations
pros: Self-motivated excels in this position. Employee drives their business with customers using their own style; Microsoft allows personal and communication skills flexed supporting strengths of personalities. Customers are typically very knowledgeable and technology-driven. Flexible work-from-home support.
cons: Yearly changes to business goals and strategies regardless of customer needs or concerns. The CSAM role is now a Sales role changing the incentives and expectations; this changes the emphasis on how best to support the customers. The CSAM role receives the "junk-drawer" of responsibilities meaning new goals and expectations are placed on the CSAM roster rather than other roles which should shoulder the tasks as they are aligned to other roles' job descriptions.
role: Former employee - CSAM - Customer Success Account Manager

title: Don't work at Azure Cloud Compute
body: Don't work at Azure Cloud Compute
pros: God pay, good stock for new grads, perks+ and other great benefits, and lots of ERGs.
cons: Azure in general has terrible WLB. It will depend team to team, but it's really frustrating to have friends in other tech companies just assuming that you have good WLB bc ur at MSFT. Even worse having friends within MSFT that are having such a chill time compared to you and getting paid the same (or maybe even more). Get away from Azure. Also, MSFT is diverse, but most people that work in Azure are either Chinese or Indian that are in work visas and can't either quit bc of that or bc they need the job to maintain their family.

title: Boring but pays the bills
pros: Good pay Amazing "work life balance". Mental health breaks, missing meetings for family events, generous time off. If you are getting work done, nobody will be on your case. Generally nice people and secure job.
cons: There's no vision in the company. Most tasks are "a pm wanted to push out a feature in 6 months so he or she could get promoted and move on to a new project." You'll be working on code that's over 30 years old. It's like being an electrician in a preserved 17th century mansion, the asks are simple but implementation is a complex chore trying to navigate such ancient codebases. 80% of engineers just do it because their parents said "engineer or doctor" and they picked engineer, they have no particular passion or competency for the work. The engineering skills you gain will be specific to microsoft or the product you are working on and will not open up opportunities at other companies. (Anyone need an x++ dev?)

title: This company gets mixed reviews
pros: Competitive Pay: Compensation is consistently strong compared to industry standards. Solid Benefits: While not as generous as in the past, benefits remain very good overall. Exciting Projects: Employees get to work on large-scale, impactful, and often fun projects that shape the tech industry. Established Reputation: Microsoft is a long-standing, globally recognized company with stability and influence. Strong Stock Performance: Company stock is highly valued and continues to be a major financial perk.
cons: Leadership Promotions: Many employees feel that the wrong people are promoted into leadership roles. Mixed Leadership Culture: Executives often say one thing but expect employees to continue working in the same old, sometimes toxic, Microsoft way. Stack Ranking: The controversial stack ranking system still exists, creating unhealthy competition. Opaque Review Process: Performance reviews lack transparency, and often the wrong individuals receive the best ratings.

title: Great tech, tough transitions â AI pivot
body: Great tech, tough transitions â AI pivot
pros: â¢ Unmatched access to cutting-edge AI tools and platforms. Microsoftâs integration of OpenAI and Azure AI into nearly every product is impressive. If youâre in a technical role, youâll be exposed to frontier innovation daily. â¢ Strong compensation for top performers. Stock awards and bonuses are generous, especially if youâre aligned with high-growth areas like Azure or Copilot. â¢ Leadership is visionary. Satya Nadellaâs strategic direction has propelled Microsoft to the forefront of the AI race, and the companyâs market cap reflects that.
cons: â¢ Massive layoffs in 2025 have shaken morale. Over 15,000 roles were cut to fund an $80B AI infrastructure push Windows C... +1. Even tenured employees werenât spared, and the restructuring felt abrupt and impersonal. â¢ Performance expectations have intensified. AI usage is now embedded in performance reviews, with mandatory adoption and tracking. This shift favors those already fluent in AI workflows, leaving others scrambling to adapt Windows Central. â¢ Cultural shifts are real. Traditional sales and marketing roles are being replaced by âsolution engineers,â and management layers are being flattened. The pace of change can feel relentless, and internal dissent is not always welcomed opentools.ai.

title: Excellent Work/Life Balance; Frequent Reorgs
pros: Microsoft is generally very good to employees, by fostering an inclusive culture, by providing challenging and engaging work, and by providing access to modern tools and technology to work with. Microsoft has a liberal leave and sick time-off policy, as well as above average (though not great) health-benefits. Finally Microsoft has not swung to far, to fast, back with RTO, but instead taken a measured and thoughtful stance.
cons: Microsoft reorganizes its workforce frequency, at least twice a year, from my perspective. This can lead to periods of uncertainty and unease leading up to (as rumors swirl) and immediately following these events. Sometimes these reorgs are logical, other times capricious; and with a lack of clear communication it can sometimes be difficult to tell which is which.

title: Wonderful place to work - but with a changing culture
body: Wonderful place to work - but with a changing culture
pros: Microsoft is absolutely unmatched for work life balance and benefits. They are focused on schilling internally and, depending on where you work. You are given time to learn and grow. People at Microsoft generally love helping each other. There is a real feeling of community within the teams.
cons: If feels like Microsoft may be losing some of its culture over the past few years. However, I feel strongly that it will get back on track. At this time. Profit is far more important then the people. Though this is a company and that is obvious , In my many years of working for them. I have always felt that the employees and customers came first. This has been shifting over the past few years. I have seen great talent leave or lose roles with no real cause given. Job security at MSFT may not be the best at this time.

title: Azure Software Engineer Review
pros: I was able to take ownership of certain product areas. Everyone on the team was really smart.
cons: Work Life balance in Azure was horrible. Oncall 3 weeks at a time, even though it's 12 hour shifts (india team takes over at night). It would be nonstop issues for the 12 hours. I started dreading the month of oncall. My pager would go off constantly, and I'd be pulled in 10 different directions. I'd have to involve my teammates when they're not on call. And when I'm not on call I'd get involved with livesite all the time as well.

title: Began wonderfully, declined over years
pros: - Lots of company benefits (wellbeing stipend, employee resource groups, funds for additional learning/certifications) - In my opinion, compared to the other biggest tech companies, Microsoft does way more charitable work and projects that contribute positively to lives around the world
cons: - Management does not live up to its espoused D&I values. Changed my pronouns during my tenure here and despite my pronouns being visible on my Teams name and email signature, and personally providing educational materials to coworkers multiple times over, NO ONE (except for other queer folks) used my proper pronouns. - No consistency in daily schedule. Meetings were constantly appearing on my calendar (or being cancelled) at the very last minute. More often than not I was on back-to-back calls the entire day, no break for lunch. East coast USA time zones always took precedent, despite headquarters being in Pacific time zone. Most days I was pressured into being on a business call beginning at 7am PT, despite being told that I had the flexibility to determine my own working hours.

title: It's alright. Corporate is not for everyone. Always keep yourself in check.
body: It's alright. Corporate is not for everyone. Always keep yourself in check.
pros: The benefits. Hands. Down. Microsoft is the stereotypical corporate company that will keep you with golden handcuffs. The benefits are fantastic, and the networking opportunities you get here are top tier. Outside of the CO+I team of Microsoft. I believe is a beautiful place. Inside CO+I, it feels like a dog-eat-dog environment.
cons: As I said earlier, Microsoft is an amazing company if you're within the right team. CO+I is a mess, but that comes with the high demand of AI across the world. The environment feels ingenuine, and I don't feel seen for the work I do. Managers don't usually interact with the technicians, and they remind us of our place constantly. We don't feel treated as equals, and the environment feels very plastic.

cons: Large and multiple waves of lay-offs along with the simultaneous top-down pressure to output twice as much is negatively impacting the work culture. The people are still great, but I can tell a lot of action is now fueled by anxiety and is rushed. Morale is at an all-time low. Also the recent return to office announcement just made things worse. Also, we have been severely understaffed for Data Scientists in our organization for years and the demand has only gotten higher.

title: The Party is over the culture is destroyed
body: The Party is over the culture is destroyed
pros: I have been with the company for a few years and they in the past at least tried to be a good company with sound morals, that is if a company can have morals but that's all out the window now.
cons: All companies have to trim head count from time to time but they have ben doing it for the last 4 years straight. With no st>opping in sight. Morale is so low that I have never seen it this bad. The company hired this new COO Carolina Dybeck Happe and ever since she has been here (since September 2024) it's been nothing but carnage. they company is moving in ways that it has never done. 1. Firing thousands of people for cause with no warning or anything. 2. because of the lawsuits spawned by number 1. They created a false offering Performance Improvement Plan (PIP) or Select Global Voluntary Separation agreement think Elon Musk and Twitter and DOGE where there is nothing voluntary about it because the PIP is not real. 3. Traditional Layoff package 4. RTO to further reduce headcount it's pretty clear to me that they have decided to eliminate a significant number of employees to pay for their AI ambitions and whatever that number is its really BIG.

title: Mileage varies
pros: Access to the latest tech, global impact, and great facilities. Many training opportunities (if you carve out the time).
cons: Extreme favoritism, unfair performance review process, and backstabbing. Many jobs quietly move overseas, so you are competing against people who live in cushy Euro job markets, regularly taking PTO while you are accountable for their work. I was told that training was on-the-job only and had to explain why I was taking free Microsoft online courses. There was no support to self-develop, while foreign employees took advantage of every type of training opportunity. Just like the rest of the US, Microsoft became a 'milking ground' for foreigners who work for the company remotely in other jurisdictions while wanting to be part of the US-made tech dream. Regular cuts.

title: Beware. Culture & People Values Permanently Damaged
body: Beware. Culture & People Values Permanently Damaged
pros: Relevant tech & most customers use some of the stack. Decent benefits but YoY belt tightening is the trend (I.e. Vision). Remote work was a benefit but RTO will eliminate that too before long. Stock growth only saving grace.
cons: Microsoft future is now driven by the new GE COO running a slash & burn, profits over people show. 30,000 employees RIFd over 2 years, Alumni on the street w little to no empathy on their departure. Those that remain â¦ the amount of hurdles one must go through to secure customer facing resources is ludicrous. Pre COVID most teams could come together some manner or you could hunt down a resource. No longer. Bloated Unified is the only way to get a resource, if youâre lucky enough to have a CSAM find one. And fewer Technical Specialists to help in every Cloud (Azure, Security, M365, Dynamics). Account teams largely dispersed and cohesion is absent. Budget for travel essentially non existent. Your individual ability to grow is largely a contest of shouting the loudest anymore. Long gone are the days of simply delivering in role. Doing whatâs right for a customer mentality.. no longer a principle unless a paid engagement is the result.

title: Fine for now
pros: Incredible smart and collaborative colleagues â you're surrounded by some of the best engineers in the world. Great compensation and benefits, including generous parental leave, 401k matching, and wellness perks. Career mobility is strong â itâs possible to switch teams, orgs, or even disciplines with manager support. Remote/hybrid flexibility has improved a lot since 2020. Culture encourages work-life balance more than you'd expect from Big Tech.
cons: Like many large companies, thereâs a lot of process, and decision-making can be slow. Performance reviews feel a bit opaque, and there's still a "stack ranking" vibe even if unofficial. Some teams can be hit-or-miss â experiences vary widely depending on your org and manager. Innovation sometimes takes a backseat to risk aversion in mature product areas.
